schools.summary: Deborah, a prophetess and the wife of Lapidoth, judging Israel: 'and Deborah, a prophetess, the wife of Lapidoth, she judged Israel at that time' — the woman raised up — 'and Deborah, a prophetess, the wife of Lapidoth, she judged Israel at that time': God raised up Deborah, a prophetess, to judge and deliver Israel (the witnesses noting the significance — the extraordinary work of God in raising up a woman, endued with the prophetic gift, to judge his people in the day of their distress, the ordinary order set aside by God's sovereign choice when he would, Deborah both prophetess, speaking God's word, and judge, deciding causes and stirring up the deliverance, a married woman yet called to public office by God's gift, the lesson — that the gifts and call of God are not bound by the natural order, the Spirit dividing to whom he will, raising up even a woman to shame the slackness of the men and to magnify his grace, 'your sons and your daughters shall prophesy,' Joel 2:28; 'she judged Israel at that time' (Deborah judging Israel, deciding causes and stirring up the deliverance, God raising up a woman to lead when the men hung back, choosing the weak to confound the mighty, the gift not bound by the order of nature, Acts 2:17; schools.summary: Barak's answer, that he would go only if Deborah went with him: 'and Barak said unto her, If thou wilt go with me, then I will go: but if thou wilt not go with me, then I will not go' — the faith mixed with weakness — 'and Barak said unto her, If thou wilt go with me, then I will go: but if thou wilt not go with me, then I will not go': Barak would obey God's command only on condition that Deborah, the prophetess, went with him (the witnesses noting the significance — a faith that is real yet weak, obeying God's call but leaning on the visible presence of the prophetess as a pledge of God's presence, the warrior's courage propped by the company of God's messenger, Barak willing to go, but not without Deborah, his faith honouring her as the token of God's word yet betraying a weakness that needed a visible support, the lesson — that even true faith is often mixed with infirmity, the believer obeying yet seeking a prop for his weakness, God bearing with the weak faith that yet obeys, 'Lord, I believe; help thou mine unbelief,' Mark 9:24, the truth that Barak is yet numbered among the faithful, his weakness not excluding him from the roll of faith, for he did obey and venture, 'by faith... Barak,' Hebrews 11:32, the figure — the imperfect faith that God accepts and uses, the weak believer who leans on the means yet ventures on the word, the trembling hand that yet takes hold, the truth that God condescends to the weakness of his servants, granting the support they crave while gently rebuking the weakness, the figure of the imperfect faith that obeys yet leans on a visible support, the call to venture on God's word even with a trembling faith, the warning that to demand a prop may forfeit some of the honour, the figure of the imperfect faith that obeys yet leans on a visible support, faith mixed with weakness); Barak willing to obey but only if Deborah went with him, his faith leaning on the prophetess as the token of God's presence (the faith mixed with weakness, Mark 9:24) — the condition (if thou go with me); 'but if thou wilt not go with me, then I will not go' (Barak refusing to go without Deborah, the weakness that needed a visible support, yet a true faith that obeyed and is numbered among the faithful, the weak faith that yet obeys, Hebrews 11:32) — the figure (the imperfect faith that God accepts and uses, the providence, the condition, Deborah's answer that the honour would go to a woman to follow, vv.9) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

sides.position: The imperfect faith that obeys yet leans on a visible support, faith mixed with weakness - a faith real yet weak, obeying God's call but leaning on the prophetess as a pledge of God's presence (Mark 9:24), Barak yet numbered among the faithful for he did venture (Hebrews 11:32), a figure of the imperfect faith God accepts and uses, the trembling hand that yet takes hold

schools.summary: Deborah's word that the honour of the victory would go to a woman, for the LORD would sell Sisera into a woman's hand: 'and she said, I will surely go with thee: notwithstanding the journey that thou takest shall not be for thine honour; for the LORD shall sell Sisera into the hand of a woman. And Deborah arose, and went with Barak to Kedesh' — the honour given to a woman — 'and she said, I will surely go with thee: notwithstanding the journey that thou takest shall not be for thine honour; for the LORD shall sell Sisera into the hand of a woman': Deborah grants Barak's request but foretells that the chief honour will fall to a woman (the witnesses noting the significance — the gentle rebuke of Barak's weakness and the sovereign disposing of God, who would give the crowning honour of the victory not to the hesitant warrior but to a woman, that human pride might be humbled and his grace magnified, the honour withheld from Barak for his leaning, and given to Jael the woman who would slay Sisera, the glory of the deed taken from the man and given to the weaker vessel, the lesson — that God humbles the pride and self-reliance of men, giving the honour where he will, often to the weak and unlikely, that no flesh should glory, 'that no flesh should glory in his presence,' 1 Corinthians 1:29, the truth that the LORD raises up the lowly and casts down the proud, the honour of his deliverances his to bestow, 'he hath put down the mighty from their seats, and exalted them of low degree,' Luke 1:52, the figure — the divine wisdom that confounds the strong by the weak, the woman's hand given the victory the warrior craved, a Jael where a Barak faltered, the truth that the glory of every deliverance is God's, who gives the honour to faith wherever found and humbles the self-reliant, the figure of the humbling of human pride and the honour God gives to faith by the weak and unexpected, the call to seek God's honour rather than our own, the warning that the prop we lean on may cost us the crown, the figure of the humbling of human pride and the honour God gives to faith, often by the weak and unexpected); Deborah granting the request but foretelling that the honour would not be Barak's, his leaning costing him the chief glory (the pride humbled, 1 Corinthians 1:29) — the word (not for thine honour); 'for the LORD shall sell Sisera into the hand of a woman. And Deborah arose, and went with Barak to Kedesh' (the LORD selling Sisera into the hand of a woman, the crowning honour given to the weaker vessel, God humbling the proud and exalting the lowly, the glory his to bestow, the lowly exalted, Luke 1:52) — the figure (the divine wisdom that confounds the strong by the weak, the providence, the word, the gathering at Kedesh and the battle to follow, vv.10) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the LORD selling Sisera into the hand of a woman signify?

sides.position: The humbling of human pride and the honour God gives to faith by the weak and unexpected - the gentle rebuke of Barak's weakness, God giving the crowning honour not to the hesitant warrior but to a woman that human pride might be humbled and no flesh should glory (1 Corinthians 1:29), the lowly exalted and the proud cast down, the divine wisdom that confounds the strong by the weak (Luke 1:52)

schools.summary: Deborah's word of faith, that the LORD had already delivered Sisera and gone out before Barak: 'and Deborah said unto Barak, Up; for this is the day in which the LORD hath delivered Sisera into thine hand: is not the LORD gone out before thee? So Barak went down from mount Tabor, and ten thousand men after him' — the assurance of faith — 'and Deborah said unto Barak, Up; for this is the day in which the LORD hath delivered Sisera into thine hand: is not the LORD gone out before thee?': Deborah, by the Spirit, declares the victory already won and the LORD himself leading the battle (the witnesses noting the significance — the boldness and certainty of faith, which speaks of the victory as already given because God has promised it and gone before, the deliverance counted done before the sword is drawn, the LORD himself marching at the head of his people's host, the present tense of faith, 'the LORD hath delivered,' the victory sure because God leads, 'is not the LORD gone out before thee?', the lesson — that faith goes forth to the battle assured of victory because God himself goes before, the cause his and the triumph certain, 'if God be for us, who can be against us?' Romans 8:31, the truth that the LORD goes out before his people to fight their battles, the captain of their salvation leading the way, 'the LORD your God... he shall fight for you,' Deuteronomy 1:30, the figure — the presence of God going before his people into every conflict, fulfilled in Christ the Captain who leads his church to victory, 'the LORD of hosts is with us,' Psalm 46:11, the truth that the seasonable word of faith stirs up the wavering to venture all on God's promise, the day of the LORD's deliverance to be seized, the figure of the faith that goes forth assured of victory because God leads the battle, the call to go up in the strength of the God who goes before, the assurance that where God leads, the victory is already won, the figure of the faith that goes forth assured of victory because God leads the battle); Deborah by the Spirit declaring the victory already given, the deliverance counted done before the battle, faith speaking in the present tense (the assurance of faith, Romans 8:31) — the word (this is the day); 'is not the LORD gone out before thee? So Barak went down from mount Tabor, and ten thousand men after him' (the LORD himself gone out before Barak to fight the battle, the captain of their salvation leading, fulfilled in Christ who leads his church to victory, Barak going down at the word, the LORD who fights for his people, Deuteronomy 1:30; Psalm 46:11) — the figure (the presence of God going before his people into conflict, the providence, the word, the LORD discomfiting Sisera to follow, vv.15) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does Deborah's word 'is not the LORD gone out before thee' establish?

sides.position: The faith that goes forth assured of victory because God leads the battle - the boldness of faith counting the deliverance done before the sword is drawn because God has promised and gone before (Romans 8:31), the LORD going out before his people to fight their battles (Deuteronomy 1:30), a figure of Christ the Captain who leads his church to victory (Psalm 46:11)

schools.summary: That the LORD himself discomfited Sisera and all his host before Barak: 'and the LORD discomfited Sisera, and all his chariots, and all his host, with the edge of the sword before Barak; so that Sisera lighted down off his chariot, and fled away on his feet' — the victory God's work — 'and the LORD discomfited Sisera, and all his chariots, and all his host, with the edge of the sword before Barak; so that Sisera lighted down off his chariot, and fled away on his feet': the LORD himself threw Sisera and his whole host into confusion, so that the captain fled on foot (the witnesses noting the significance — the victory wrought wholly by God, who discomfited the foe before Barak's sword ever told, the rout the act of heaven and not of the warriors, the nine hundred iron chariots and the great host scattered by the hand of the LORD, the discomfiting God's own work, by storm and flood as the song tells, the strength of man overthrown before the battle was joined, 'the LORD discomfited Sisera,' the lesson — that the victory of God's people is his gift and his deed, the foe routed by heaven and not by their own might, 'there is no king saved by the multitude of an host: a mighty man is not delivered by much strength,' Psalm 33:16, the truth that the strongest array of man is nothing before God, the chariots of iron scattered as chaff, 'some trust in chariots, and some in horses: but we will remember the name of the LORD,' Psalm 20:7, the figure — the God who fights for his people and overthrows their enemies, the captain who scatters the foe before the battle, fulfilled in Christ who conquers his people's enemies, the truth that the great Sisera, the terror of twenty years, fled on foot, his pride brought to nothing by God, the figure of the victory wrought by God and not by the sword of man, the foe routed by the hand of heaven, the call to trust the God who routs the foe, the assurance that no array of man can stand before the LORD, the figure of the victory wrought by God, not by the sword of man, the foe routed by the hand of heaven); the LORD himself discomfiting Sisera and his whole host before Barak's sword told, the rout the act of heaven (the victory God's work, Psalm 33:16) — the discomfiting (the LORD routed them); 'so that Sisera lighted down off his chariot, and fled away on his feet' (Sisera, the terror of twenty years, fleeing on foot, his pride and chariots brought to nothing by God, the strongest array nothing before the LORD, the chariots scattered, Psalm 20:7) — the figure (the God who fights for his people and overthrows their enemies, the providence, the rout, Barak pursuing the host to follow, vv.16) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does 'the LORD discomfited Sisera' establish about the victory?

sides.position: The victory wrought by God, not by the sword of man, the foe routed by the hand of heaven - the rout God's own work, scattering the foe before Barak's sword told, the victory his gift and deed (Psalm 33:16), the strongest array of man nothing before God, the chariots scattered as chaff (Psalm 20:7), the great Sisera fleeing on foot with his pride brought to nothing

themes: And the LORD discomfited Sisera, and all his chariots, and all his host, with the edge of the sword before Barak; so that Sisera lighted down off his chariot, and fled away on his feet
that the LORD himself discomfited Sisera and all his host before Barak (the crux of the victory wrought by God, not by the sword of man)
the rout wrought wholly by God, who scattered the foe before Barak's sword told, the nine hundred chariots and the great host overthrown by the hand of heaven
the strongest array of man nothing before God, the great Sisera fleeing on foot with his pride brought to nothing (Psalm 33:16; Psalm 20:7)

schools.summary: Barak pursuing and the whole host of Sisera falling, not a man left: 'but Barak pursued after the chariots, and after the host, unto Harosheth of the Gentiles: and all the host of Sisera fell upon the edge of the sword; and there was not a man left' — Barak pursued and destroyed the whole host, none left (the but Barak pursued after the chariots, and after the host, unto Harosheth of the Gentiles, the pursuit, the and all the host of Sisera fell upon the edge of the sword; and there was not a man left, the host destroyed, the figure, Barak completing the victory God had wrought, the host utterly destroyed, the truth that the whole host of Sisera fell, the victory complete, Judges 4:7, the figure of the complete destruction of the foe God had delivered, none escaping, the pursuit of Barak, the destruction of the host, vv.15); Barak pursuing the host (the pursuit, Judges 4:7) — the pursuit (to Harosheth); 'all the host of Sisera fell upon the edge of the sword; and there was not a man left' (the host utterly destroyed, the victory complete, the figure, the complete destruction of the foe God had delivered, the providence, the destruction, Sisera fleeing to Jael's tent to follow, vv.17) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: But Barak pursued after the chariots, and after the host, unto Harosheth of the Gentiles: and all the host of Sisera fell upon the edge of the sword; and there was not a man left
Barak pursuing and the whole host of Sisera falling, not a man left
Barak completing the victory God had wrought, the whole host of Sisera utterly destroyed
none escaping (Judges 4:7)

schools.summary: Sisera fleeing to Jael's tent, where there was peace with Jabin: 'howbeit Sisera fled away on his feet to the tent of Jael the wife of Heber the Kenite: for there was peace between Jabin the king of Hazor and the house of Heber the Kenite' — Sisera fled to Jael's tent, trusting the peace between Jabin and Heber (the howbeit Sisera fled away on his feet to the tent of Jael the wife of Heber the Kenite, Sisera fled to Jael's tent, the for there was peace between Jabin the king of Hazor and the house of Heber the Kenite, the peace, the figure, Sisera fleeing to what he thought a refuge, the providence leading him to his death, the truth that Sisera, trusting the peace, fled to the tent where he would die, Judges 4:9, the figure of the false refuge that becomes the place of death, the foe led to his ruin, the flight of Sisera, the false refuge, vv.9); Sisera fleeing to Jael's tent (the flight, Judges 4:9) — the flight (to Jael's tent); 'there was peace between Jabin the king of Hazor and the house of Heber the Kenite' (the peace that made Sisera trust the tent, the false refuge, the figure, the false refuge that becomes the place of death, the providence, the flight, Jael coming out to meet Sisera to follow, vv.18) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Howbeit Sisera fled away on his feet to the tent of Jael the wife of Heber the Kenite: for there was peace between Jabin the king of Hazor and the house of Heber the Kenite
Sisera fleeing to Jael's tent, where there was peace with Jabin
Sisera fleeing to what he thought a refuge, trusting the peace
the providence leading him to the place where he would die (Judges 4:9)

schools.summary: Jael inviting Sisera in and covering him with a mantle: 'and Jael went out to meet Sisera, and said unto him, Turn in, my lord, turn in to me; fear not. And when he had turned in unto her into the tent, she covered him with a mantle' — Jael welcomed Sisera, bidding him not fear, and hid him (the and Jael went out to meet Sisera, and said unto him, Turn in, my lord, turn in to me; fear not, the welcome, the and when he had turned in unto her into the tent, she covered him with a mantle, hidden, the figure, Jael's welcome and concealment, the foe drawn into the snare, the truth that Jael, by her welcome, drew Sisera into security, the providence working his ruin, Judges 4:9, the figure of the foe lulled into false security before his fall, the welcome of Jael, the foe hidden, vv.17); Jael welcoming Sisera (the welcome, Judges 4:9) — the welcome (turn in, fear not); 'she covered him with a mantle' (Sisera hidden under the mantle, the snare, the figure, the foe lulled into false security, the providence, the welcome, Sisera asking for water to follow, vv.19) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And Jael went out to meet Sisera, and said unto him, Turn in, my lord, turn in to me; fear not. And when he had turned in unto her into the tent, she covered him with a mantle
Jael inviting Sisera in and covering him with a mantle
Jael welcoming Sisera and hiding him, the foe drawn into the snare
lulled into false security before his ruin (Judges 4:9)

schools.summary: Sisera asking water and Jael giving him milk: 'and he said unto her, Give me, I pray thee, a little water to drink; for I am thirsty. And she opened a bottle of milk, and gave him drink, and covered him' — Sisera asked water, and Jael gave him milk and covered him again (the and he said unto her, Give me, I pray thee, a little water to drink; for I am thirsty, Sisera asks water, the and she opened a bottle of milk, and gave him drink, and covered him, Jael gives milk, the figure, Jael giving more than was asked, the milk that lulled him to sleep, the kindness that masked the snare, the truth that Jael gave milk for water, the drink that brought on sleep, Judges 5:25, the figure of the seeming kindness that prepared the foe's ruin, the foe lulled to sleep, the milk of Jael, the foe lulled, vv.18); Sisera asking for water (the request) — the request (a little water); 'she opened a bottle of milk, and gave him drink, and covered him' (Jael giving milk that lulled him, the snare, Judges 5:25, the figure, the kindness that masked the snare, the providence, the milk, Sisera bidding her stand at the door to follow, vv.20) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And he said unto her, Give me, I pray thee, a little water to drink; for I am thirsty. And she opened a bottle of milk, and gave him drink, and covered him
Sisera asking water and Jael giving him milk
Sisera asking water and Jael giving milk, the drink that lulled him to sleep
the kindness that masked the snare (Judges 5:25)

schools.summary: Sisera bidding Jael guard the door and deny his presence: 'again he said unto her, Stand in the door of the tent, and it shall be, when any man doth come and enquire of thee, and say, Is there any man here? that thou shalt say, No' — Sisera bade Jael guard the door and deny that he was there (the again he said unto her, Stand in the door of the tent, stand at the door, the and it shall be, when any man doth come and enquire of thee, and say, Is there any man here? that thou shalt say, No, deny his presence, the figure, Sisera trusting Jael to hide him, secure in his false refuge, the truth that Sisera, secure and weary, slept while he trusted the woman to guard him, Judges 4:21, the figure of the foe secure in his false refuge, trusting the very hand that would slay him, the charge to Jael, the false security, vv.18); Sisera bidding Jael guard the door (the charge) — the charge (stand at the door); 'thou shalt say, No' (Jael to deny his presence, the false security, Judges 4:21, the figure, the foe trusting the hand that would slay him, the providence, the charge, Jael taking the tent nail to follow, vv.21) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Again he said unto her, Stand in the door of the tent, and it shall be, when any man doth come and enquire of thee, and say, Is there any man here? that thou shalt say, No
Sisera bidding Jael guard the door and deny his presence
Sisera bidding Jael guard the door and deny his presence, secure and weary
trusting the very woman who would slay him (Judges 4:21)

schools.summary: Jael driving the tent nail through Sisera's temples as he slept: 'then Jael Heber's wife took a nail of the tent, and took an hammer in her hand, and went softly unto him, and smote the nail into his temples, and fastened it into the ground: for he was fast asleep and weary. So he died' — the judgment of God by a lowly hand — 'then Jael Heber's wife took a nail of the tent, and took an hammer in her hand, and went softly unto him, and smote the nail into his temples, and fastened it into the ground: for he was fast asleep and weary. So he died': Jael, with a tent nail and hammer, slew the sleeping Sisera (the witnesses noting the significance — the overthrow of the great oppressor by the hand of a woman with the meanest of weapons, the tools of the tent, the proud captain of nine hundred chariots brought down not in battle but in sleep by a lowly hand, the judgment of God fulfilling Deborah's word that Sisera would be sold into a woman's hand, the deed variously weighed: by some as Jael's own act, neither wholly to be praised nor a pattern, the breach of hospitality and the bloodshed left to God's secret judgment, by most as the appointed instrument of God's righteous judgment on his enemy, Jael blessed in Deborah's song, 'blessed above women shall Jael... be,' Judges 5:24, the deed owned as God's work though the means be strange, the lesson — that God brings down the proud and mighty by the weak and unexpected, the oppressor's pride ending in a tent by a woman's hand, 'he hath put down the mighty from their seats,' Luke 1:52, the truth that the enemies of God's people fall by his judgment, the means often the lowliest, that the glory be his alone, the figure — the certain ruin of the proud foe of God's church, the head of the serpent bruised, the oppressor of God's people destroyed by God's appointed means, the truth that the long oppression of twenty years ended in a moment by a tent nail, God's judgment sure and his word fulfilled, the figure of the fall of the proud oppressor by an unexpected and lowly hand, the judgment of God, the call to trust the God who brings down the mighty, the warning to the enemies of God's people that their fall is certain); Jael taking the tent nail and hammer and driving it through the sleeping Sisera's temples, the great captain slain by the meanest of weapons (the proud brought low, Luke 1:52) — the deed (the nail in his temples); 'and fastened it into the ground: for he was fast asleep and weary. So he died' (Sisera, fast asleep and weary, slain by Jael's hand, the appointed instrument of God's judgment fulfilling Deborah's word, the oppressor of twenty years ended in a moment, Jael blessed in the song, the judgment of God, Judges 5:24) — the figure (the certain ruin of the proud foe of God's church, the providence, the death, Barak coming and Jael showing him Sisera to follow, vv.22) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: How is Jael's slaying of Sisera with the tent nail to be understood?

sides.position: The appointed instrument of God's righteous judgment on his enemy - the proud oppressor brought down by a lowly hand fulfilling Deborah's word that Sisera would be sold into a woman's hand, God bringing down the mighty by the weak (Luke 1:52), Jael blessed above women in the song as the means of God's judgment (Judges 5:24)

A deed left to God's secret judgment, owned as his instrument yet not a pattern to imitate - the breach of hospitality and the bloodshed not simply to be praised, the act God overruled for his righteous ends though the means be strange

themes: Then Jael Heber's wife took a nail of the tent, and took an hammer in her hand, and went softly unto him, and smote the nail into his temples, and fastened it into the ground: for he was fast asleep and weary. So he died
Jael driving the tent nail through Sisera's temples as he slept (the crux of the fall of the proud oppressor by an unexpected and lowly hand, the judgment of God)

the overthrow of the great oppressor by the hand of a woman with the meanest of weapons, the proud captain brought down in sleep by a lowly hand, fulfilling the word that Sisera would be sold into a woman's hand
God bringing down the mighty by the weak (Luke 1:52), the enemies of God's people falling by his judgment, Jael blessed in the song (Judges 5:24)

schools.summary: Jael showing Barak the dead Sisera: 'and, behold, as Barak pursued Sisera, Jael came out to meet him, and said unto him, Come, and I will shew thee the man whom thou seekest. And when he came into her tent, behold, Sisera lay dead, and the nail was in his temples' — Jael showed Barak the dead Sisera, the nail in his temples (the and, behold, as Barak pursued Sisera, Jael came out to meet him, and said unto him, Come, and I will shew thee the man whom thou seekest, Jael shows Barak, the and when he came into her tent, behold, Sisera lay dead, and the nail was in his temples, Sisera dead, the figure, the proof of the victory, the foe dead and the word fulfilled, the honour to a woman, the truth that Barak, who sought Sisera, found him slain by a woman, Deborah's word fulfilled, Judges 4:9, the figure of the word of God fulfilled, the foe dead by the appointed hand, the revealing of Sisera, the word fulfilled, vv.9); Jael showing Barak the man he sought (the proof, Judges 4:9) — the revealing (I will shew thee); 'Sisera lay dead, and the nail was in his temples' (Sisera dead, the word fulfilled, the honour to a woman, the figure, the word of God fulfilled by the appointed hand, the providence, the revealing, God subduing Jabin to follow, vv.23) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And, behold, as Barak pursued Sisera, Jael came out to meet him, and said unto him, Come, and I will shew thee the man whom thou seekest. And when he came into her tent, behold, Sisera lay dead, and the nail was in his temples

schools.summary: God subduing Jabin before Israel: 'so God subdued on that day Jabin the king of Canaan before the children of Israel' — God subdued Jabin the oppressor before Israel (the so God subdued on that day Jabin the king of Canaan before the children of Israel, God subdued Jabin, the figure, God himself subduing the oppressor, the victory his work, the truth that the subduing of Jabin was God's deed, the deliverance his, Psalm 44:3, the figure of the God who subdues the enemies of his people, the victory ascribed to him, the subduing of Jabin, the deliverance of God, vv.15); God subduing Jabin (the deliverance, Psalm 44:3) — the subduing (Jabin); 'before the children of Israel' (the oppressor subdued before Israel, the victory God's, the figure, the God who subdues the enemies of his people, the providence, the subduing, Israel prevailing until Jabin was destroyed to follow, vv.24) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: So God subdued on that day Jabin the king of Canaan before the children of Israel

schools.summary: Israel prevailing until Jabin was destroyed: 'and the hand of the children of Israel prospered, and prevailed against Jabin the king of Canaan, until they had destroyed Jabin king of Canaan' — Israel prospered and prevailed until Jabin was wholly destroyed (the and the hand of the children of Israel prospered, and prevailed against Jabin the king of Canaan, Israel prevailed, the until they had destroyed Jabin king of Canaan, Jabin destroyed, the figure, Israel pressing the victory to the full, the oppressor wholly destroyed, the deliverance complete, the truth that the victory was followed up until Jabin was destroyed, the deliverance full, Judges 5:31, the figure of the complete deliverance, the foe wholly subdued, the prevailing of Israel, the destruction of Jabin, vv.23); Israel prospering and prevailing against Jabin (the conquest, Judges 5:31) — the prevailing (against Jabin); 'until they had destroyed Jabin king of Canaan' (Jabin wholly destroyed, the complete deliverance, the figure, the complete deliverance, the foe wholly subdued, the providence, the destruction, the song of Deborah and Barak to follow, ch.5) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.
